He *encloses* us because we *see* with his fundamental perceptions."[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloom2008xii-273) In the 21st century, his reputation remains pre-eminent. [Emma Smith](/wiki/Emma_Smith_(scholar) "Emma Smith (scholar)"), professor of Shakespeare Studies at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), in a study published in 2019, called him "the world's greatest playwright";[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2019cover-274) while [Dennis Kennedy](/wiki/Dennis_Kennedy_(author) "Dennis Kennedy (author)"), Samuel Beckett Professor of Drama and Theatre (Emeritus) at [Trinity College Dublin](/wiki/Trinity_College_Dublin "Trinity College Dublin"), noted in 2004 that he remains "the most performed playwright in the world".[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy20042-275) [Gary Taylor](/wiki/Gary_Taylor_(scholar) "Gary Taylor (scholar)"), co-editor of *[The New Oxford Shakespeare](/wiki/The_Oxford_Shakespeare "The Oxford Shakespeare")* published in 2017, wrote; "most of the most important, the most talented, and best educated writers and readers of the last four centuries have considered Shakespeare the best writer in English, or the best modern writer in any language, or the world's best playwright, or the best Western writer of the last thousand years, or the best writer ever, or the greatest genius of all time".[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorBourus20171-276)

## Speculation

### Authorship

Main article: [Shakespeare authorship question](/wiki/Shakespeare_authorship_question "Shakespeare authorship question")

Around 230 years after Shakespeare's death, doubts began to be expressed about the authorship of the works attributed to him.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapiro201077–78-277) Proposed alternative candidates include [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon"), [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe") and [Edward de Vere, 17th Earl of Oxford](/wiki/Edward_de_Vere,_17th_Earl_of_Oxford "Edward de Vere, 17th Earl of Oxford").[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibson200548,_72,_124-278) Several "group theories" have also been proposed.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMichaelGlenn196256-279) All but a few Shakespeare scholars and literary historians consider it a fringe theory, with only a small minority of academics believing that there is reason to question the traditional attribution,[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_New_York_Times2007-280) but interest in the subject, particularly the [Oxfordian theory of Shakespeare authorship](/wiki/Oxfordian_theory_of_Shakespeare_authorship "Oxfordian theory of Shakespeare authorship"), continues into the 21st century.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKathman2003620,_625–626-281)[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELove2002194–209-282)[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchoenbaum1991430–440-283)

### Religion

Main article: [Religious views of William Shakespeare](/wiki/Religious_views_of_William_Shakespeare "Religious views of William Shakespeare")

Shakespeare conformed to the official state religion,[[k]](#cite_note-285) but his private views on religion have been the subject of debate. [Shakespeare's will](/wiki/Shakespeare%27s_will "Shakespeare's will") uses a Protestant formula, and he was a confirmed member of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), where he was married, his children were baptised, and where he is buried.

Some scholars are of the view that members of Shakespeare's family were Catholics, at a time when practising Catholicism in England was against the law.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPritchard19793-286) Shakespeare's mother, [Mary Arden](/wiki/Mary_Shakespeare "Mary Shakespeare"), certainly came from a pious Catholic family. The strongest evidence might be a Catholic statement of faith signed by his father, [John Shakespeare](/wiki/John_Shakespeare "John Shakespeare"), found in 1757 in the rafters of his former house in Henley Street. However, the document is now lost and scholars differ as to its authenticity.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood200375–78-287)[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckroyd200622–23-288) In 1591 the authorities reported that John Shakespeare had missed church "for fear of process for debt", a common Catholic excuse.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood200378-289)[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckroyd2006416-290)[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchoenbaum198741–42,_286-291) In 1606 the name of William's daughter Susanna appears on a list of those who failed to attend Easter [communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") in Stratford.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood200378-289)[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckroyd2006416-290)[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchoenbaum198741–42,_286-291)

Other authors argue that there is a lack of evidence about Shakespeare's religious beliefs. Scholars find evidence both for and against Shakespeare's Catholicism, Protestantism, or lack of belief in his plays, but the truth may be impossible to prove.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson200434-292)[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapiro2005167-293)

In 1934, [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling") published a short story in *[The Strand Magazine](/wiki/The_Strand_Magazine "The Strand Magazine")*, "Proofs of Holy Writ", postulating that Shakespeare had helped to polish the prose of the [King James Bible](/wiki/King_James_Bible "King James Bible"), published in 1611.[[283]](#cite_note-294)

### Sexuality

Main article: [Sexuality of William Shakespeare](/wiki/Sexuality_of_William_Shakespeare "Sexuality of William Shakespeare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Shakespeare%27s_family_circle.jpg/250px-Shakespeare%27s_family_circle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shakespeare%27s_family_circle.jpg)

Artistic depiction of the Shakespeare family, late 19th century

Few details of Shakespeare's sexuality are known. At 18 he married 26-year-old [Anne Hathaway](/wiki/Anne_Hathaway_(wife_of_Shakespeare) "Anne Hathaway (wife of Shakespeare)"), who was pregnant. Susanna, the first of their three children, was born six months later on 26 May 1583. Over the centuries, some readers have posited that Shakespeare's sonnets are autobiographical,[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee190055-295) and point to them as evidence of his love for a young man. Others read the same passages as the expression of intense friendship rather than romantic love.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasey1998-296)[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPequigney1985-297)[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans1996132-298) The 26 so-called ["Dark Lady"](/wiki/Dark_Lady_(Shakespeare) "Dark Lady (Shakespeare)") sonnets, addressed to a married woman, are taken as evidence of heterosexual liaisons.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFort1927406–414-299)

### Portraiture

Main article: [Portraits of Shakespeare](/wiki/Portraits_of_Shakespeare "Portraits of Shakespeare")

No written contemporary description of Shakespeare's physical appearance survives, and no evidence suggests that he ever commissioned a portrait. From the 18th century, the desire for authentic Shakespeare portraits fuelled claims that various surviving pictures depicted Shakespeare.[[289]](#cite_note-300) That demand also led to the production of several fake portraits, as well as misattributions, re-paintings, and relabelling of portraits of other people.[[290]](#cite_note-301)[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchoenbaum1981190-302)

Some scholars suggest that the [Droeshout portrait](/wiki/Droeshout_portrait "Droeshout portrait"), which [Ben Jonson](/wiki/Ben_Jonson "Ben Jonson") approved of as a good likeness,[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooper200648,_57-303) and his [Stratford monument](/wiki/Shakespeare%27s_funerary_monument "Shakespeare's funerary monument") provide perhaps the best evidence of his appearance.[[293]](#cite_note-304) Of the claimed paintings, the art historian [Tarnya Cooper](/wiki/Tarnya_Cooper "Tarnya Cooper") concluded that the [Chandos portrait](/wiki/Chandos_portrait "Chandos portrait") had "the strongest claim of any of the known contenders to be a true portrait of Shakespeare". After a three-year study supported by the [National Portrait Gallery, London](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery,_London "National Portrait Gallery, London"), the portrait's owners, Cooper contended that its composition date, contemporary with Shakespeare, its subsequent provenance, and the sitter's attire, all supported the attribution.[[294]](#cite_note-305)
